message: The ANTISCAM dataset contains 220 human-human dialogs (average conversation length 12.45 turns and average utterance length 11.13 words).

Intent is annotated hierarchically by separating on-task and off-task information: on-task uses task-related intents, while off-task is annotated with dialog acts (e.g., "open question") that convey syntactic information.
TransferTransfo and the hybrid model.

Automatic metrics: Perplexity; Response-Intent Prediction (RIP) and Response-Slot Prediction (RSP) computed by comparing system-intent and slot predictions (the intent predictor achieves 84% accuracy and the slot predictor 77% on the ANTI-SCAM dataset); and Extended Response-Intent Prediction (ERIP) and Extended Response-Slot Prediction (ERSP) which score predicted intents/slots by including acceptable alternatives estimated via transition probabilities. Human evaluation metrics: Fluency, Coherence, Engagement, Length and Task Success.
It is competitive with other systems with access to larger data sources for some tasks.
They compare against Liu et al. (2015) and Yang et al. (2012).

Yes — they created their own dataset consisting of 202 confirmed rumours from Sina Weibo’s official rumour debunking service and 202 non-rumours gathered via the Sina Weibo API, along with 200 news articles from Xinhua News Agency.
Chinese (Sina Weibo).
Unconfirmed (novel) information with respect to trusted resources is considered as an indication of rumours.
They compared with LDA, Doc-NADE, HTMM, and GMNTM.
Generative model evaluation (test set perplexity) and document classification.
CoNLL2003, OntoNotes 5.0, OntoNotes 4.0 (Chinese), MSRA, Weibo, and Resume.

They replace the vanilla sinusoidal absolute position encoding with a directional, relative positional encoding (adopting and revising prior relative positional encodings) so the attention is both direction- and distance-aware, using a revised formulation that uses fewer parameters.
Yes — on six NER datasets they achieve state-of-the-art performance among models without considering pre-trained language models or designed features.

The contributions are: (i) the adaptation of a state-of-the-art sequence-to-sequence (seq2seq) neural system to event detection and classification for Italian in a single-step approach; (ii) an investigation on the quality of existing Italian word embeddings for this task; (iii) a comparison against a state-of-the-art discrete classifier; and the release of the pre-trained models and scripts.

They used a random baseline of 0.20 for the Word Intrusion task. They used the cluster centroid as a strong baseline label for the cluster-labeling task.

Yes — the experiments and analyses consider both derivational and inflectional morphology; for example, Turkish data are split at derivational boundaries while inflectional groups are represented as separate tokens, and the paper distinguishes effects of derivational versus inflectional morphemes.

Gold morphological features are used, where morph outputs the lemma of the token followed by language-specific morphological tags (and for some languages additional information such as parts-of-speech tags, e.g., for Turkish).

They used the Semantic Scholar corpus (SemScholar), Springer Nature’s SciGraph, the Textbook Question Answering corpus, the January 2018 English Wikipedia dataset, and the Flickr30K and COCO image-sentence matching benchmarks.

whether the text is direct or indirect; if it is offensive, disrespectful, hateful, fearful out of ignorance, abusive, or normal; the attribute based on which it discriminates against an individual or a group of people; the name of this group; and how the annotators feel about its content within a range of negative to neutral sentiments.
Their dataset contains 13,000 tweets.

Semantic Web technologies can make the semantics of content explicit so that machines can use this knowledge, empowering MT systems to supply translations with significantly better quality while remaining scalable. In particular, disambiguated knowledge about real-world entities, their properties and their relationships made available on the Linked Data Web can be used to infer the right meaning of ambiguous sentences or words.

Structural divergence (reordering errors); linguistic properties/features (complex tense systems, grammatical gender and morphology); and the five open problems identified by Lopez and Post: excessive focus on English/European languages and poor research on low-resource pairs, limitations of SMT across domains, translating large amounts of non-standard social‑media speech, difficult translations among morphologically rich languages, and mismatched parallel data for speech translation.
They extracted five statistical features: root mean square, zero crossing rate, moving window average, kurtosis and power spectral entropy.

They are compared by evaluating how effectively crowd-sourced and expert-annotated graphs can represent the multimodal structure of diagrams for representation learning using various graph neural networks. The results show that the identity of diagram elements can be learned from their layout features, while the expert annotations provide better representations of diagram types.

They use Wikipedia’s "List of Controversial articles" as seed and crawl the "See also", "References" and "External links" hyperlinks up to two hops from those seeds, assuming labels propagate (a page linked from a controversial issue is labeled controversial).
Yes — they compare neural/semantic models to lexical baselines including TfIdf-SVM, language models (LM-wiki, LM-DBPedia) and the TILE-Clique matching model.
The training data consisted of about 64M segments from YouTube videos comprising a total of 1.2B tokens.

They allow experts to specify anchor terms (relevance variables) and incorporate them by combining the information bottleneck with CorEx so that latent factors are encouraged to be informative about both correlations in the data and the expert-specified relevance variables.

They are a sentence-level baseline and a concatenative baseline (concatenating each sentence with the preceding sentence).

They experimented with concat22 (concatenating each sentence with the preceding sentence), s-hier (multi-encoder with hierarchical attention over the previous source), s-t-hier (multi-encoder over previous source and previous target), and s-hier-to-2 (additional encoder for source context with target concatenated); for each variant they also tested weight tying (sharing embedding parameters between encoders reading the same kind of text).
English and German.

They run an EL algorithm for the current mention; if it returns an entity they retrieve its Freebase types, map them to the target type set and one-hot encode them as the KB type representation (using an empty set if NIL), and they also include the EL algorithm's confidence score as a feature.
A deep neural model that uses two layers of BiLSTMs to encode the context and a three-layer MLP classifier, concatenating context, mention string, KB type representation, and the EL confidence score.
They evaluate on FIGER (GOLD) and BBN.
One.
Precision, recall, F1-score, support, and accuracy (including accuracy without the ‘O’ tag).
F1-Score 0.89; Accuracy 0.97; Accuracy without ‘O’-tag 0.86
Yes — implementation with demo and dataset is available at: https://github.com/jitinkrishnan/NASA-SE.

A token-level BIO labelling scheme (B = beginning, I = inside/continuation, O = outside) is used with domain-specific tags such as B-cardinal, I-grp, B-opcon, B-abb, B-org, B-syscon, B-event, B-seterm, B-art, B-mea, B-loc, etc.
BERT (the pre-trained cased language model).
The constructed CR dataset contains roughly 3,700 sentences annotated at the word-token level.
Precision, recall and F-measure.
Hand-crafted features include the position of the sentence, sentence length and tense, as well as content features, qualifying adjectives and meta-discourse features.
Word2vec-derived embeddings using three models: averaging word vectors (AVGWVEC), paragraph vectors / PV-DM (PARAVEC), and BAS-specific word embeddings (BSWE).
No; they use existing annotated corpora (e.g., the Argumentative Zoning Corpus as the AZ-annotated test dataset and ACL Anthology collection for training) rather than annotating their own dataset.

Sentence embeddings were generated using three models: averaging word vectors (AVGW VEC), where word2vec-learned word embeddings are averaged per sentence; paragraph vectors (PARAVEC / PV-DM), which trains distributed memory paragraph vectors; and BAS-specific word embeddings (BSWE) trained (inspired by sentiment-specific embeddings) and then averaged per sentence.
Argumentative zoning (AZ) is rhetorical sentence classification, a process of assigning rhetorical status to the extracted sentences.

They crawled two credit-based freemium blackmarket sites (YouLikeHits and Like4Like) between February and April 2019, created dummy accounts (after IRB approval) to participate and recorded Tweet IDs of tweets posted for gaining retweets, and then used Twitter’s REST API to collect the tweet objects and the timelines of the authors.

They compare to state-of-the-art Twitter spam detection methods (Spam Detection 1: Wu et al. [5]; Spam Detection 2: Rajdev et al. [12]) and feature-concatenation classifiers (Random Forest, MLP, SVM).
English
They look at FreeFollowers, Like4Like, YouLikeHits, and JustRetweet.
English, Chinese, and French.
The decoder is based on the Transformer architecture as part of a Transformer sequence-to-sequence model.
The encoder is a bidirectional encoder.
XLM (Lample and Conneau 2019), a state-of-the-art cross-lingual pre-trained Transformer.

They use Amazon Mechanical Turk human evaluations: a triple-pairing prompt/story task (groups of three stories with shuffled prompts where judges select the correct pair; 105 stories per model evaluated by 15 judges each) to measure prompt-story pairing accuracy, and a blind pairwise comparison to evaluate hierarchical vs non-hierarchical generation (400 pairs evaluated by 5 judges each) reporting human preference percentages.
Perplexity and prompt ranking accuracy.

They compare against (1) Language Models: non-hierarchical models (GCNN and their additional self-attention mechanism), (2) seq2seq: LSTMs and convolutional seq2seq architectures including Conv seq2seq with decoder self-attention, (3) Ensemble: an ensemble of two Conv seq2seq with self-attention models, and (4) KNN: a KNN model using FASTTEXT and FAISS to retrieve the closest prompt's story (retrieved story limited to 150 words).
No; the paper does not use BERT—instead they train a fusion mechanism on top of a pre-trained seq2seq model and use 300-dimensional embeddings.

The extracted features are partitioned into six groups: numerical features, language models, word clusters, topic models, part-of-speech (POS) tags, and essay text (bigram bag-of-words with idf weighting).

The dataset contained 8,262 annotated "bot" and "non-bot" Twitter users split 80%/20% into 6,610 training users (61,160,686 tweets) and 1,652 held-out test users (15,703,735 tweets). For feature extraction they used up to each user’s 1,000 most recent tweets for topic modeling and the dataset was manually annotated for bot/non-bot labels.

They created PICONET by collecting structured abstracts from PubMed (search on April 11, 2019 yielding 363,078 structured abstracts filtered by Article Types: Clinical Trial, Species: Humans, Languages: English) and carefully choosing abstract headings representative of Population, Intervention, and Outcome. They lemmatized section labels to cluster similar headings, manually inspected samples to select candidate labels (avoiding keyword-based selection), kept full abstract sections (not splitting multi-sentence sections), added negative examples from headings like "aim", and cleaned the data (language identification, removal of missing values, unicode cleaning, and filtering sequences between 5 and 200 words).
The dataset was automatically labeled using structured abstract section headings from PubMed, with the authors manually inspecting a small number of samples to determine representativeness.
Redundancy and ambiguity.

The sentence construction component relies on the GF library: the GF grammar encoder takes the sentence structure and components identified earlier and selects/apply GF rules (from the assigned rules and extended rules) to produce constructors and operators for each sentence. The GF grammar exporter then unions the per-sentence grammars into a final GF program which GF linearizes to generate the sentences.
It compares with the system used in the Phylotastic project and an ontology about people.

No; they focus on continuous vector representations of languages (language vectors) learned with a character-based neural language model and analyze the structure of the language space (e.g. hierarchical clustering showing genetic relationships), rather than studying how word representations vary across languages.
A character-based neural language model using two LSTM layers, with language vectors concatenated to the inputs of the LSTMs and the final softmax.
By performing hierarchical clustering on the learned language vectors; Figure 5 shows a hierarchical clustering of Germanic languages that closely matches established genetic relationships.

Definition of ad hoc data structures; Preprocessing of the original ADR description; Word-by-word linear scan of the description and voting task; Weight calculation and term ranking; Selection of the best terms.

By building a meta-dictionary hash table (DictByWord and DictByWordStem) of all words occurring in MedDRA that maps each word to the set of LLTs containing it (built in O(mk) time where m is the number of LLTs and k the length of the longest LLT) and loading the MedDRA dictionary into hash tables. The algorithm then proceeds with a word-by-word iteration on the preprocessed text, testing each token against the hash tables in constant time, yielding overall linear complexity in the size of the narrative and the terminology.

The baseline is a one-stage RNN system (trained only with dialect category labels) whose RNN structure matches the last stage: a 2-layer BLSTM directly trained to recognize the dialect category.

The two-stage system achieves 88.88% overall accuracy compared to the baseline 78.85% (three-stage 87.24%), a relative increase of about 10%. Both multi-stage systems outperform the baseline, with the two-stage system performing best overall, excelling in long-duration (>3s, 90.04%) and showing robustness in short-duration (≤3s, 87.72%) tasks.
